design_philosophy.core_loop: Use pinball mechanics (flipper control, ball physics) to progress through RPG-style castle levels, defeat enemies by hitting targets, earn gold, purchase upgrades at Black Market, advance to next stage
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing traditional pinball skill execution with RPG progression goals; managing limited resources (gold) for strategic upgrade purchases while maintaining ball control
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Rescue Princess Bali from Beezelbub by piloting a magical silver pinball through a six-level castle, combining the tactile satisfaction of pinball with narrative RPG progression
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical genre fusion treating pinball as a viable RPG progression mechanic rather than a separate minigame; commitment to 'pinball as narrative vehicle' with castle stages, enemy encounters, and story beats delivered through pinball table design rather than cutscenes. Described as 'among the most bizarre genre hybrids in video game history' and 'baffling experiment' - the game doubles down on this identity rather than apologizing for it

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can purchase upgraded flippers and stoppers at Black Market using accumulated gold, allowing players to invest resources to improve their mechanical control and performance on subsequent attempts

progression.upgrade_system: Gold-based equipment progression: flippers and stoppers can be purchased at Black Market run by imps; upgrades improve pinball control mechanics
progression.unlock_mechanism: Six castle stages unlock sequentially: Tomb → Gate → Goblins → River → Harpy → Throne room (final boss Beezelbub). Player must accomplish objectives on each stage's pinball table to advance
replayability_hooks: Three additional game modes beyond RPG (Circus slot machine mode, Viva! Golf whack-a-mole mode, Pop! Pop! 4-player sports mode)
Multiplayer support for Pop! Pop! mode
Gold collection and upgrade purchasing creates replay incentive to optimize resource gathering

world_building_techniques: Stage-based castle progression (thematic level design: Tomb, Gate, Goblins, River, Harpy, Throne room)
Enemy/character encounters embedded in pinball table design (Captain's Spirit, Ziffroo witch, goblin guards, toitles, harpies, Beezelbub)
US regional addition: intro cutscene showing queen kidnapped and king beaten, establishing rescue narrative before gameplay begins
Instruction manual lists notable characters and features for each stage, providing context for thematic table design

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters treated as pinball table challenges; defeating enemies involves hitting specific targets on the table rather than traditional combat. Each stage culminates in a boss encounter (Beezelbub final boss) integrated into pinball mechanics
notable_bosses: Beezelbub (Dark Lord of the Machine) - final throne room boss

faction_indicators: Black Market run by imps - morally ambiguous merchant faction
danger_signals: Castle setting with escalating enemy types (skeletons, demons, goblins, dark knights, harpies)
Progressive stage difficulty through enemy variety
environmental_storytelling: Castle architecture progression reflects narrative (Tomb → Gate → Goblins → River → Harpy → Throne room hierarchy)
Pinball table aesthetics match stage themes (skeletal imagery for Tomb, witch elements for Gate, etc.)
Gold currency as material evidence of conquest/progression through stages

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Pinball Quest article (developer credit TOSE, regional differences, unused graphics context)
Wikipedia - Pinball Quest article (gameplay modes, RPG mode stage list, character names, narrative setup, critical reception context)
